name: Pure Olive Oil
shortDescription: Pure olive oil, specifically the first pressing, held immense ritual and symbolic significance in ancient Jewish tradition, particularly within the Temple and Tabernacle.
description: Pure olive oil played a crucial role in ancient Jewish religious practices, primarily as fuel for the menorah in the Tabernacle and later in the Temple in Jerusalem. Its purity was paramount; only the first pressing of the olives was considered suitable for sacred use. This 'pure beaten olive oil' (shemen zayit zach katit le-ma'or) symbolized purity, light, and divine presence. The menorah itself, a seven-branched candelabrum, represented the Tree of Life and the spreading of divine wisdom. The constant burning of the olive oil in the menorah signified God's eternal presence and the continuous pursuit of knowledge and understanding. Beyond illumination, olive oil was also used in various offerings and anointing rituals, marking individuals or objects as sacred and set apart for God's purpose. The process of obtaining the oil, particularly the first pressing, was meticulously controlled to ensure its ritual purity. The anointing oil used to consecrate priests and kings also contained olive oil as a primary ingredient, further highlighting its importance in establishing divine authority and spiritual leadership. The menorah, fueled by pure olive oil, served as a central symbol of Jewish identity and faith, representing the enduring covenant between God and the Jewish people. The use of olive oil connects to ideas of consecration, divine light, and the ongoing relationship between humanity and the divine, playing a critical role in the ritual life surrounding the Tabernacle and Temple.

sources.title: The Temple: Its Ministry and Services
sources.author: Alfred Edersheim
